article.title: NASA Targets 6:24 PM EDT Launch for Artemis II, the First Crewed Lunar Mission in Over 53 Years
article.summary: Four astronauts are set to lift off aboard the Space Launch System from Kennedy Space Center on a 10-day voyage around the Moon, marking humanity's return to deep space.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

NASA is preparing to launch Artemis II on Wednesday, April 1, sending four astronauts on a 10-day journey around the Moon and back to Earth in what will be the first crewed mission beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 in December 1972. The launch window opens at 6:24 p.m. EDT at Kennedy Space Center's Launch Complex 39B, with an [80 percent chance of favorable weather conditions](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/). If the rocket does not lift off today, backup windows are available each day through April 6.

This follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-03/29-artemis-ii-crew-arrives-at-kennedy-space-center-as-countdown-begins-for-first-crewed-lunar-voyage-in-53-years) on the crew's arrival at the launch site on March 27 and [prior coverage of NASA's Artemis program overhaul](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028) announced in late February.

## The Crew

The four-person crew represents several historic firsts. Commander Reid Wiseman, 50, a former U.S. Navy test pilot who previously spent 165 days aboard the International Space Station, will lead the mission. Pilot Victor Glover, 49, a Navy aviator, is the [first Black astronaut assigned to a lunar mission](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch). Mission Specialist Christina Koch, 47, holds NASA's record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days. Canadian Space Agency astronaut Jeremy Hansen, 50, a former Royal Canadian Air Force fighter pilot on his first spaceflight, will become the [first Canadian to travel beyond Earth orbit](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

"Hey, let's go to the Moon! I think the nation and the world has been waiting a long time to do this again," Commander Wiseman stated, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## Mission Profile

Artemis II is a systems validation mission designed to test the Orion spacecraft's life support, navigation, communications, and propulsion systems with a crew aboard for the first time. The Space Launch System rocket will generate nearly 9 million pounds of thrust at liftoff, sending the 5.7 million-pound vehicle toward orbit at nearly five miles per second, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

The crew will travel approximately 685,000 miles over the course of the mission, reaching a distance of 252,000 miles from Earth and passing within roughly 4,100 miles of the lunar surface on a free-return trajectory, as reported by [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1). The astronauts will observe previously unseen regions of the Moon's far side before heading back to Earth.

Upon reentry, the crew will return at approximately 25,000 mph, which [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1) notes would make them the fastest humans in history, surpassing the record set by Apollo 10 in 1969. Splashdown is targeted for April 10 in the Pacific Ocean, with 11 parachutes slowing the capsule to a landing velocity of 15 to 17 mph, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/).

## Launch Readiness

NASA reported on March 31 that final prelaunch preparations are proceeding on schedule. The RS-25 engines completed critical health checks confirming that sensors, connections, and diagnostics are operational. The Orion spacecraft's flight batteries have been brought to full charge for avionics, life support, and communications systems, and astronaut pressure suit regulator leak checks have been completed, [verifying airtight seals](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/).

Non-essential personnel departed Launch Complex 39B on the evening of March 31, with the ground launch sequencer set to activate in the early morning hours. The primary weather concerns are cumulus clouds, ground winds, and solar weather, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/). NASA and the U.S. Space Force Space Launch Delta 45 will monitor conditions continuously through the launch window.

Tanking operations coverage begins at 7:45 a.m. EDT on NASA's YouTube channel, with full launch coverage starting at 12:50 p.m. on NASA+, Amazon Prime, and YouTube, [NASA confirmed](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/03/31/nasa-teams-readying-artemis-ii-moon-rocket-for-launch/).

## What We Don't Know

The mission's path to the launch pad has not been smooth. Artemis II faced two significant technical delays in early 2026: a liquid hydrogen leak detected during a February wet dress rehearsal and a helium flow issue discovered in March, as reported by [Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch). Whether these recurring issues indicate deeper engineering challenges with the SLS platform has not been formally assessed.

The broader Artemis timeline also remains in flux. Following a [major program restructuring announced in late February](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028), the actual lunar landing has been pushed to Artemis IV in 2028, with Artemis III redesigned as an Earth orbit test flight in 2027. Whether NASA can achieve its stated goal of increasing launch cadence to roughly one mission every ten months after years of multi-year gaps between flights remains an open question.

## Significance

Artemis II is the second flight of the Space Launch System and the first crewed mission of the Orion spacecraft. Its success is a prerequisite for every subsequent step in NASA's lunar exploration plans, including the eventual return of astronauts to the Moon's surface near the south pole, where water ice deposits could support a sustained human presence. The mission also serves as a proving ground for technologies that NASA intends to use for eventual crewed missions to Mars, as noted by [Al Jazeera](https://www.aljazeera.com/news/2026/4/1/what-is-nasas-artemis-ii-moon-mission-and-when-will-it-launch).
